title: Answers to Week 3 Hands On Deep Dive Deep Learning in Python INFS3822
body:
INFS3822 – AI for Business Analytics

Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Deep Dive - Deep Learning in Python Build a Credit Card Fraud Detector using Deep Learning in Python and Keras/Tensorflow

In this hands-on Deep Dive activity, we would like to predict if a credit card transaction is genuine or fraudulent. To do that, we will train an AI model using a dataset of anonymised credit card transactions made by European cardholders for 2 days in September 2013. Source: https://www.kaggle.com/mlg-ulb/creditcardfraud. Transactions are labelled as either fraudulent or genuine. the 284,807 transactions in the dataset, 492 are fraudulent, (making up 0.172% of all transactions). For this Hands-On activity, we will use a smaller sample of 20,000 transactions provided on Moodle as a CSV file called ‘creditcard_sample.csv’. Due to confidentiality issues, the original features and background information about the data are not provided. Instead, the dataset includes features labeled V1, V2, ..., V28, which are numeric values resulting from a PCA transformation. Additionally, the 'Time' feature represents the seconds elapsed between each transaction and the first transaction in the dataset, while 'Amount' indicates the transaction amount. The 'Class' feature is the response variable, taking the value 1 for fraud and 0 for genuine transactions. Given that the response variable has two possible values- is binary (0 for genuine and 1 for fraud), we will create a binary classification Neural Network using TensorFlow, an open-source platform for machine learning. We will employ the Keras package in Python/R, a widely used deep learning framework built on top of TensorFlow, to simplify the creation of neural network models. Load the Dependencies The initial step involves defining the functions and classes (dependencies) required for building your machine learning model. We will use the ‘read_csv’ function from the pandas library to load the dataset. In addition, we will also use two classes from the Keras library to define your machine learning model. First, we need to load the necessary libraries and functions to build our machine learning model.

• pandas: For loading and handling the dataset. • tensorflow.keras: For building the neural network model. • sklearn.metrics: For evaluating the performance of the model. • sklearn.preprocessing: For scaling the input data.

import pandas as pd from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential from tensorflow.keras.layers import Dense from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix from sklearn.metrics import classification_report from sklearn import preprocessing

Load the data CRICOS Provider Code 00098G

First, download the dataset from Moodle, which is available as a CSV file named ‘creditcard_sample.csv’. After downloading, upload the CSV file to your Colab notebook.

The response variable in the dataset has two possible values: 0 for genuine transactions and 1 for fraudulent transactions, making this a binary classification problem. The other input variables describing each transaction are numerical, which is ideal for neural networks that require numerical input and output values. Let's load the dataset from the CSV file using the following code: dataset = pd.read_csv('creditcard_sample.csv')

Prepare the data

The dataset contains 30 input variables and one output variable (the last column). Our objective is to train a ML model that maps the rows of input variables (X) to the output variable (y).

We split the data into input variables (X) and the output variable (y).

• X: All columns except 'Class'. • y: The 'Class' column, which indicates whether a transaction is fraudulent (1) or genuine (0).

# Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y) X = dataset.drop(columns=['Class']) y = dataset["Class"].astype(bool)

To ensure that all input features are on the same scale, we scale the numeric columns. This helps the neural network to learn more effectively. It standardises the data by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation.

# Scale the numeric input data # Identify numeric columns numeric_columns = X.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64']).columns

# Scale numeric columns scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X[numeric_columns]) X[numeric_columns] = scaler.transform(X[numeric_columns]) Define the model In Keras, models are defined as a sequence of layers. We create a Sequential model and add layers one at a time to achieve the desired network architecture. It is crucial to ensure that the input layer has the correct number of input features, specified using the ‘input_shape’ argument. For our model, we set ‘input_shape’ to ‘(30,)’, representing the 30 input variables as a vector. In this example, we will use a fully connected network structure with three layers. Fully connected layers are defined using the Dense class, where the number of neurons or nodes in the layer is specified as the first argument, and the activation function is specified using the activation argument. We will use the rectified linear unit (relu) activation function for the first two layers and the sigmoid function for the output layer.

We define a Sequential model in Keras and add layers to it. The input layer has 30 features, and we use three fully connected layers with the following specifications.

The layers can be combined by adding each one sequentially as follows:

• The model expects rows of data with 30 variables (the input_shape=(30,) argument).

• First hidden layer: 12 nodes, 'relu' activation function. • Second hidden layer: 6 nodes, 'relu' activation function. • Output layer: 1 node, 'sigmoid' activation function (since this is a binary classification problem).

Let's define the keras model in Python: # Define the model model = Sequential() model.add(Dense(12, input_shape=(30,), activation='relu')) model.add(Dense(6, activation='relu')) model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))

Compile the model Now that the model is defined, the next step is to compile it. Compiling the model involves specifying the loss function used to evaluate a set of weights, the optimizer used to search through different weights for the network, and any metrics we want to track during training. These elements help the model learn the optimal set of weights to map inputs to outputs in the dataset. For this binary classification problem, we will use cross-entropy as the loss function, which is defined in Keras as ‘binary_crossentropy’. For the optimizer, we select ‘adam’, a popular version of gradient descent that automatically adjusts its parameters and performs well across various problems. Finally, since this is a classification task, we will evaluate the model's performance using classification accuracy, specified via the metrics argument.

Compiling the model involves setting the loss function, optimizer, and metrics.

• Loss function: 'binary_crossentropy' (suitable for binary classification problems). • Optimizer: 'adam' (an efficient optimizer for training deep learning models). • Metrics: 'accuracy' (to measure the performance of the model).

Let's compile the model as follows: # Compile the model model.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer='adam', metrics=['accuracy'])

Train the model We train the model on our dataset. The training involves multiple epochs ((iterations through the dataset), set via the ‘epochs’ argument. Also, we must specify the batch size, which determines the number of dataset rows considered before the model weights are updated within each epoch. This is set using the ‘batch_size’ argument.

• Epochs: Number of times the model will see the entire dataset. • Batch size: Number of samples per gradient update.

In this example, we will train the model for a small number of epochs 5 epochs and use a relatively small batch size of 200.

Let’s train the model as follows:

# Train the model model.fit(X, y, epochs=5, batch_size=200)

Evaluate the model

After successfully training the neural network model on the entire dataset, we need to evaluate the performance of the model in making predictions to check how well it performs on the training data. The ‘evaluate()’ function helps us do this by calculating the loss and accuracy of the model.

The evaluate ‘evaluate()’ function from Keras takes the input data (X) and the corresponding labels (y) to calculate the loss and accuracy.

It returns two values: the first is the loss (which we can ignore for now), and the second is the accuracy. For our purpose of this activity, we are primarily interested in reporting the accuracy, so we can disregard the loss value.

# Evaluate the model _, accuracy = model.evaluate(X, y) print('Accuracy: %.2f' % (accuracy*100))

Accuracy: 99.74

Evaluate the Model: The model.evaluate(X, y) function runs the model on the given inputs (X) and outputs (y).

The underscore _ is used to ignore the first returned value (loss), and accuracy stores the second returned value.

Print the Accuracy: The print('Accuracy: %.2f' % (accuracy*100)) line formats and prints the accuracy as a percentage with two decimal places.

accuracy*100 converts the accuracy from a fraction (like 0.9974) to a percentage (like 99.74).

Let’s Interpret Accuracy for Our Neural Network to assess its performance for predicting whether a credit card transaction is genuine or fraudulent.

Accuracy is a metric used to measure how well a model makes predictions. It is calculated as the ratio of correctly predicted instances to the total number of instances. In simpler terms, it indicates the percentage of times the model's predictions were correct.

Model Performance When we say our neural network model has an accuracy of 99.74%, it means that the model correctly identifies whether a transaction is genuine or fraudulent 99.74% of the time based on the input data.

Let’s suppose we evaluate 1000 transactions using our trained model. With 99.74% accuracy, the model will correctly classify approximately 997 out of 1000 transactions. This includes both correctly identifying genuine transactions and correctly identifying fraudulent transactions.

With 99.74% accuracy, the model will incorrectly classify approximately 3 out of 1000 transactions. This includes genuine transactions incorrectly flagged as fraudulent (false positives) and fraudulent transactions incorrectly flagged as genuine (false negatives). Let’s say we have a dataset of 1000 transactions, what does accuracy of 99.74% mean? Correctly Classified Transactions (99.74%) • Genuine Transactions: The model correctly identifies 997 transactions as genuine. • Fraudulent Transactions: The model correctly identifies 997 transactions as fraudulent. Incorrectly Classified Transactions (0.26%)

• False Positives: Suppose 1 genuine transaction is incorrectly classified as fraudulent. • False Negatives: Suppose 2 fraudulent transactions are incorrectly classified as genuine.

Accuracy equals 99.74 means that the model correctly predicts whether a transaction is fraudulent or genuine 99.74% of the time. By evaluating the model in this way, you can determine how well it has learned to classify the transactions based on the training data. Why Accuracy matters? Accurate detection of fraudulent transactions helps mitigate financial losses for both customers and businesses. When our model has 99.74% accuracy, it means that almost all fraudulent transactions are caught, and very few get missed. This high accuracy helps protect both customers and businesses from losing money to fraud. It also means that genuine transactions are less likely to be mistakenly flagged as fraud, which makes the system more reliable and trustworthy for users.

Also, High accuracy in fraud detection builds customer trust in the system. Customers feel more secure knowing their transactions are accurately monitored for fraud. In addition, Manual review teams can focus on the small number of transactions flagged by the model, making the fraud detection process more efficient and reducing the workload on human analysts.

Make Predictions After training and evaluating the model, the next step is to use it to make predictions on our data. The predict() function generates probabilities for each transaction being fraudulent or genuine. Since we use a sigmoid activation function in the output layer, these probabilities will range between 0 and 1. A higher probability indicates a higher likelihood of the transaction being fraudulent. We convert these probabilities into binary predictions (0 or 1). o If the probability is less than 0.5, we classify it as 0 (genuine). o If the probability is 0.5 or greater, we classify it as 1 (fraudulent). The following code allows to make predictions: # Make class predictions with the model predictions = (model.predict(X) > 0.5).astype(int)

Then we display the confusion matrix, which is a table that helps understand the performance of our classification model by showing the number of correct and incorrect predictions.

True Positives (TP): Transactions that are actually fraudulent and predicted as fraudulent. True Negatives (TN): Transactions that are actually genuine and predicted as genuine. False Positives (FP): Transactions that are actually genuine but predicted as fraudulent (also known as Type I error). False Negatives (FN): Transactions that are actually fraudulent but predicted as genuine (also known as Type II error).

We use the confusion_matrix function to generate this matrix:

# Print confusion matrix print(confusion_matrix(y, predictions)) [[19886 29] [ 23 62]]

Let’s interpret the results of the Confusion Matrix

• 19886: True Negatives (TN) - The model correctly predicted 19886 genuine transactions. • 29: False Positives (FP) - The model incorrectly predicted 29 genuine transactions as fraudulent.

• 23: False Negatives (FN) - The model incorrectly predicted 23 fraudulent transactions as genuine. • 62: True Positives (TP) - The model correctly predicted 62 fraudulent transactions.

We can also calculate the Accuracy, Precision, Recall (sensitivity), F1 score and specificity using the ‘classification_report’ function.

print(classification_report(y, predictions))

Let’s interpret the Results Class 0 (Genuine Transactions) For genuine transactions, the model has a precision of 1.00, which means that 100% of the transactions it predicted as genuine were indeed genuine. The recall is also 1.00, indicating that the model correctly identified 100% of the actual genuine transactions. The F1 score for genuine transactions is 1.00, showing a perfect balance between precision and recall.

Class 1 (Fraudulent Transactions) For fraudulent transactions, the model's precision is 0.68, meaning that 68% of the transactions it predicted as fraud were actually fraudulent. The recall is 0.73, indicating that the model correctly identified 73% of the actual fraudulent transactions. The F1 score for fraudulent transactions is 0.70, which balances both precision and recall, giving us a single measure of the model's effectiveness in detecting fraud.

The overall accuracy of the model is 0.99, meaning that 99% of all the transactions were correctly classified as either genuine or fraudulent. By understanding these metrics, you can see how well the model performs in detecting fraudulent transactions while also accurately identifying genuine transactions. Evaluate feature Importance using Permutation Importance Feature importance refers to techniques that assign a score to input features based on how useful they are in predicting a target variable. Permutation importance is one technique. It quantifies the decrease in a model's performance when the values of a feature are randomly shuffled. This breaks the relationship between the feature and the target variable, allowing to see how much the model relies on that feature.

• High Scores: Features with high importance scores are crucial for the model. Changing their values significantly impacts the model's performance. • Low Scores: Features with low importance scores have little impact on the model's predictions. • Negative Scores: These indicate features that may be redundant, noisy, or causing overfitting.

Please note that negative feature importance scores indicate that certain features may be redundant, noisy, or causing overfitting in the model.

Import Necessary Libraries:

We import ‘numpy’ for numerical operations and ‘permutation_importance’ from ‘sklearn.inspection’ to calculate permutation importance.

import numpy as np from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance

Create a Wrapper for the Keras Model We define a class KerasClassifierWrapper to wrap our Keras model. This allows us to use it with scikitlearn functions. The class has methods to fit the model, make predictions, and score the model.

# Wrapper for Keras model class KerasClassifierWrapper(BaseEstimator, ClassifierMixin): def __init__(self, model): self.model = model

def fit(self, X, y): self.model.fit(X, y, epochs=1, batch_size=50, verbose=0) return self

def predict(self, X): return (self.model.predict(X) > 0.5).astype(int)

def score(self, X, y): _, accuracy = self.model.evaluate(X, y, verbose=0) return accuracy

We create an instance of the wrapper class with our trained model

# Create an instance of the wrapper wrapped_model = KerasClassifierWrapper(model)

Calculate Permutation Importance We use the permutation_importance function to calculate the importance of each feature. This function evaluates how much the model's accuracy decreases when the values of each feature are randomly shuffled.

# Calculate permutation importance results = permutation_importance(wrapped_model, X, y, scoring='accuracy', n_repeats=3, random_state=42)

Summarise Feature Importance We loop through each feature and print its importance score. Higher scores mean the feature is more important for the model.

# Summarize feature importance for i in range(X.shape[1]): print(f'Feature: {X.columns[i]}, Importance: {results.importances_mean[i]:.4f}')

Feature: Time, Importance: 0.0001 Feature: V1, Importance: 0.0010

Feature: V2, Importance: 0.0005 Feature: V3, Importance: 0.0008 Feature: V4, Importance: 0.0020 Feature: V5, Importance: 0.0002 Feature: V6, Importance: 0.0001 Feature: V7, Importance: 0.0001 Feature: V8, Importance: 0.0005 Feature: V9, Importance: 0.0002 Feature: V10, Importance: 0.0003 Feature: V11, Importance: 0.0002 Feature: V12, Importance: 0.0006 Feature: V13, Importance: 0.0001 Feature: V14, Importance: 0.0039 Feature: V15, Importance: 0.0002 Feature: V16, Importance: 0.0003 Feature: V17, Importance: 0.0009 Feature: V18, Importance: 0.0002 Feature: V19, Importance: 0.0003 Feature: V20, Importance: 0.0000 Feature: V21, Importance: 0.0002 Feature: V22, Importance: 0.0001 Feature: V23, Importance: 0.0000 Feature: V24, Importance: 0.0001 Feature: V25, Importance: 0.0001 Feature: V26, Importance: 0.0009 Feature: V27, Importance: 0.0006 Feature: V28, Importance: 0.0001 Feature: Amount, Importance: 0.0001

High Importance Features Features like V4 (Importance: 0.0020) and V14 (Importance: 0.0039) have higher importance scores, meaning the model relies heavily on these features to make accurate predictions. If we shuffle their values, the model's performance drops significantly.

Low Importance Features Features like Time (Importance: 0.0001) and Amount (Importance: 0.0001) have low importance scores, meaning the model does not rely much on these features. Shuffling their values does not significantly impact the model's performance.

By evaluating feature importance, we can understand which features are most crucial for the model's predictions. This helps in refining the model by focusing on important features and possibly removing redundant or noisy ones.
